free_text: Cross sections of deuteron induced reactions on 45Sc
up to 50 MeV: Experiments and comparison with
theoretical codes
The Sc targets were prepared from commercially
available high purity Sc foils (Goodfellow, 99.5%
purity) with nominal thickness of 0.100 mm.
Two stacks were
irradiated with 50 and 20 MeV deuteron beams. The
high energy stack contained 20 Sc foils together with
27 micron thick Al monitor foils and foils for energy
degradation. The low energy stack contained 11 Sc
foils and 10 Ni foils for monitoring. The 50 MeV
irradiation was done at LLN for 40 min with 115nA
beam current, the 20 MeV nominal deuteron beam
irradiation at VUB was performed took place with a
constant 110nA beam current for 65 min. The activity
produced in the targets and monitor foils was
measured non-destructively (without chemical
separation) using a high resolution HpGe gamma ray
spectrometer coupled to a Canberra-Genie acquisition
system.
Chapter 4
for Ni
The uncertainty of the energy of the
bombarding beam in the middle of each irradiated foil
was estimated combining the uncertainties of the
energy degradation depending on the thickness of the
target foils in the stack and the energy straggling.
The uncertainty on the median energy in the last foil
was +-1.1 MeV for the low energy irradiation, while it
amounted to +-1.3 MeV for the last foil of the high
energy irradiation.
The uncertainties of the cross-section
values were estimated in the standard way: the
independent relative errors of the linearly
contributing processes were summed quadratically and
the square root of the sum was taken. The average
uncertainty was found  to be 10-13%.
surface density of target nuclei (5%);
incident charged particle flux (5%),
energy dependent detector efficiency (5%),
nuclear decay and branching data (5%)
statistical and peak fitting (1-8%)
The experimental data were compared to the
results of the theoretical model codes TALYS, EPIRE-D
and ALICE-D.
The measured experimental data were compared to
those found in the literature.
Fitted excitation functions were used for
determination of integral physical yields.
